one_line_takeaway: A decade-spanning survey that taxonomizes adversarial attacks, defenses, and certified-robustness methods and ties them to automated-driving perception tasks and the SOTIF safety framework — synthesis and taxonomy, with no experiments of its own.
key_finding: As a survey the paper reports no new empirical numbers of its own; its central synthesized claims are qualitative: DNN-based perception across all major automated-driving tasks (traffic-sign recognition, object/vehicle detection, semantic segmentation, LiDAR and sensor-fusion 3D perception, trajectory prediction, SLAM) is broadly vulnerable to adversarial examples, spanning both digital imperceptible ℓp perturbations and real-world physical attacks (adversarial patches, printable perturbations governed by a non-printability score, LiDAR spoofing/malicious-obstacle generation). It argues that exact certified defenses (e.g., Reluplex/SMT-based formal verification) give tight guarantees but do not scale to large models, while approximate methods (IBP bound propagation, Lipschitz-based, randomized smoothing, convex relaxation) trade tightness for scalability. Its distinctive framing is that adversarial robustness should be integrated into the SOTIF safety process — treating universal adversarial perturbations and rapid adversarial training as tools to stress-test and improve perception-system safety rather than only as external threats.
core_contribution: This is a survey (a section of the LNCS "Automated Driving Vehicle Technologies" book) by Jun Yan and Huilin Yin (Tongji University) that systematically reviews a decade of adversarial-robustness research, with a specific focus on environment-perception systems for automated driving. Its own contribution is organizational and synthetic rather than experimental: it (1) lays out theory preliminaries (gradient-based attacks, adversarial training, randomized smoothing) with formal notation; (2) taxonomizes attack methods into digital white-box/black-box and physical attacks (Tables 2 and 3); (3) taxonomizes defenses, including a large catalog of certified/robust-verification methods grouped by mechanism — formal verification/SMT, mixed-integer programming, convex relaxation, bound propagation (IBP), Lipschitz continuity, randomized smoothing, uncertainty-based, and cybernetics-based — each labeled exact vs. approximate and whether it scales; (4) maps these to concrete automated-driving perception tasks (object detection, semantic segmentation, 3D/LiDAR perception, trajectory prediction); and (5) connects adversarial robustness to the Safety of the Intended Functionality (SOTIF) framework and outlines future directions (foundation models like Segment-Anything, state-space/Mamba architectures, edge/quantized deployment, robustness-uncertainty-privacy integration).
threat_model: Adversarial attacks against DNN-based environment-perception models in automated driving, operating primarily at inference time. Threats surveyed include: digital imperceptible ℓp-bounded perturbations (white-box gradient-based and black-box score-/decision-based), and physical-world attacks (robust printable adversarial patches, maliciously modified traffic signs, and LiDAR/sensor-fusion spoofing that injects or hides obstacles). Attacker goals span targeted and untargeted misclassification/mis-detection across sign recognition, object detection, segmentation, 3D perception, and trajectory prediction. Note: the paper frames this as a review of the collective literature's threat landscape rather than proposing a single new threat model.
limitations: It is a narrative survey, not an empirical study: it offers no reproducible experiments, no unified benchmark, and no quantitative comparison of the many methods it catalogs, so relative effectiveness must be taken on the cited works' word. Coverage is breadth-first and citation-dense, and some emerging areas it flags (ViT/foundation-model robustness, black-box trajectory-prediction attacks, edge/quantized deployment) are noted as open rather than analyzed. As a book-chapter review its taxonomy labels (e.g., exact vs. approximate, scalable yes/no) are high-level and not always justified with evidence in-text.

datasets_used: No datasets are used by the survey itself (it runs no experiments)
Datasets referenced only as they appear in surveyed prior works: MNIST, CIFAR-10, GTSRB, ImageNet (classification/verification), and KITTI, nuScenes (LiDAR/3D autonomous-driving benchmarks)
quantitative_results: The survey reports no quantitative results of its own — no accuracy, robust-accuracy, mIoU, attack-success-rate, or certified-radius numbers are measured by the authors
Tables 2 and 3 and the certified-defense table are method taxonomies/catalogs (e.g., attack type, exact vs. approximate, mechanism, scalable yes/no), not empirical benchmark tables
The only quantitative object shown is a citation-count growth trend of adversarial-robustness papers (Fig. 1, credited to Nicholas Carlini's blog), used to motivate the field's scale
baselines_compared: None — as a survey it performs no head-to-head experimental comparison; methods are compared descriptively/taxonomically, not benchmarked by the authors
